comments.text: M. Dębski and M. Śleszyńska-Nowak have shown in this paper(2022) that the maximum size of a strong clique in a circle graph with maximum degree $\Delta$ is at most $\frac{5}{4} \Delta^2$. Note: The bound is tight for even $\Delta$ and correct up to a smaller order error term for odd $\Delta$. Also, [Sl15] is unable to load any reference. Edit: A special case of this conjecture has been settled by P. Horak, H. Qing and W.T. Trotter in "Induced matchings in cubic graphs"(1993). They show that the edge set of a cubic graph can always be partitioned into 10 subsets, each of which induces a matching in the graph. The same result was proven independently by Andersen in "The strong chromatic index of a cubic graph is at most 1"(1992). (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

The result discussed in the Master's thesis of Mohammad Mahdian implies this conjecture of Erdos and Nešetřil for graphs without a 4-cycle. Other problems on chromatic numbers have been discussed here too: https://utoronto.scholaris.ca/server/api/core/bitstreams/db30068f-ef0a-4ace-b77c-e5e364ee3120/content (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Indeed, excluding a bipartite graph generally has a much stronger effect compared to excluding a non-bipartite graph. It forces the graph to become sparse (few edges; compare the Erdős-Stone theorem). So it makes sense that excluding a four-cycle changes the optimal answer a lot for this problem as well. Incidentally, Vu (2002) extended Mahdian's result as follows: $\chi(L(G)^2) \leq C_H \frac{\Delta^2}{\log \Delta}$ when a fixed bipartite graph $H$ is excluded, which is sharp up to optimizing the constant $C_H$. For cliques, a similar effect occurs; if you exclude a fixed even cycle from $G$, the best possible upper bound on $\omega(L(G)^2)$ becomes linear in $\Delta$, rather than quadratic, see e.g. Cho, Choi, Kim and Park (2021). Probably $\omega(L(G)^2)\leq C_H \Delta$ still holds when any fixed bipartite graph $H$ is excluded, but actually I can't remember right now whether that has been studied.

Equivalently, this asks whether the chromatic number of the square of the line graph $L(G)^2$ of $G$ is at most $\frac{5}{4}\Delta^2$. It is even still open whether the clique number of $L(G)^2$ is at most $\frac{5}{4}\Delta^2$. The mentioned result of Chung-Trotter and Gyárfás-Tuza constituted a proof for the special case where $G$ does not contain two strongly independent edges, i.e. if $L(G)^2$ is a complete graph. For general $G$, the current best upper bound is $\frac{4}{3} \Delta^2$. The bound $\frac{5}{4} \Delta^2$ is also sharp and true if $G$ is triangle-free. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
